figures.caption: Table 4: Evaluation results on VURB. The bold values indicate the best and underlined values indicate the second best.

Table 1: Comparison with previous benchmarks on video understanding data. Our VURB spans comprehensive video dimensions, features long chain-of-thought reasoning in all preference responses, and employs amajority-voting evaluation protocol for robust assessment.
Table 5: Evaluation results on VideoRewardBench. The bold values indicate the best and underlined values indicate second best.

Figure 6: An example from VURB. GPT-5.2 fails to identify the salient “sharp point” event in the video, leading it to incorrectly select Response 2. In contrast, VideoGRM carefully analyzes the merits of Response 1 and the errors of Response 2 by grounding its judgment in visual evidence from the video, correctly identifying Response 1 as the better response. VideoDRM also correctly selects Response 1.

Figure 1: Overview of the VURB construction pipeline. We first sample prompts from multiple sources to ensure diversity and broad task coverage (Sec. 3.1). We then generate preference responses using five multimodal models (Sec. 3.2). Finally, we apply a two-stage data quality control procedure (Sec. 3.3), including (1) length consistency control, which filters response pairs with relative length differences larger than 25%, and (2) causal consistency control, which removes samples where reasoning and final answers are inconsistent.
Table 10: Evaluation results with different N values on VURB. The bold values indicate the best and underlined values indicate the second best.
Figure 4: Best-of-𝑁 across different reward models.

Figure 3: Generative reward training with GRPO improves video understanding performance. Our GRPO-trained Qwen3VL8B-Instruct shows consistent improvements across most metrics. Notably, it achieves a significant 6.6% improvement overall accuracy on Video-Holmes. We report the result on VideoMME without subtitles, the debiased Multiple-Choice-Question set of VSI-Bench and the test split of Video-Holmes.
Table 3: Training data statistics. * denotes subsets constructed in this work. “TI2T” and “TV2T” denote Text-Image-to-Text and Text-Video-to-Text respectively.
Table 7: Prompt template for preference response generation in CoT format.
Table 8: Prompt template for answer matching.
Table 9: Prompt template for evaluation on Video Understanding Reward Bench.

Figure 2: Overview of the two reward modeling paradigms. VideoDRM appends a scalar score head to the backbone and assigns scores (𝑟chosen, 𝑟rejected) to each response, optimized via a margin-based ranking loss. VideoGRM generates multiple reasoning rollouts, optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a binary reward (1.0 for correctly identifying the chosen response, 0.0 otherwise) to produce preference decisions aligned with ground-truth labels.
